annotations.label: Functor

anchor.snippet: A functor F from C to D is a mapping that

annotations.note: The `Functor` structure bundles `obj`, `map`, `map_id` and `map_comp`, matching the article's definition exactly.

annotations.label: Contravariant functor

anchor.snippet: We then define a contravariant functor F from C to D as a mapping that

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes a contravariant functor C→D as an ordinary functor `Cᵒᵖ ⥤ D` out of the opposite category, with no separate dedicated type.

annotations.label: Variance of composite functors

anchor.snippet: The composite of two functors of the same variance

annotations.note: Mathlib handles variance via opposite categories rather than tracking variance, so the rule about composing covariant/contravariant functors is not stated as a proposition.

annotations.label: Covariant functor

anchor.snippet: Ordinary functors are also called covariant functors

annotations.note: Ordinary (covariant) functors are exactly `CategoryTheory.Functor`.

annotations.label: Cofunctor

anchor.snippet: Contravariant functors are also occasionally called cofunctors

annotations.note: A cofunctor is a contravariant functor, represented in Mathlib as a functor `Cᵒᵖ ⥤ D`.

annotations.label: Opposite functor

anchor.snippet: induces the opposite functor

annotations.note: `Functor.op` sends `F : C ⥤ D` to the induced functor `Cᵒᵖ ⥤ Dᵒᵖ`.

annotations.label: Bifunctor

anchor.snippet: A bifunctor (also known as a binary functor ) is a functor whose domain is a product category

annotations.note: Bifunctors are functors `C × D ⥤ E` out of the product category `prod`, used throughout Mathlib (e.g. `Functor.uncurry`, `curriedTensor`).

annotations.label: Multifunctor

anchor.snippet: A multifunctor is a generalization of the functor concept to n variables

annotations.note: Mathlib has bifunctors (`C × D ⥤ E`) and some trifunctor constructions (`Localization.Trifunctor`) but no general n-ary multifunctor concept.

annotations.label: Consequences of functor axioms

anchor.snippet: Two important consequences of the functor axioms are

annotations.note: The consequence that a functor preserves isomorphisms is `Functor.map_isIso`/`Functor.mapIso`; preservation of identities and composition are the axioms `map_id`/`map_comp` themselves.

annotations.label: Composition of functors

anchor.snippet: One can compose functors

annotations.note: `Functor.comp` (notation `⋙`) composes functors and is associative with identity `𝟭`.

annotations.label: One-object category as monoid

anchor.snippet: A small category with a single object is the same thing as a monoid

annotations.note: `SingleObj M` is the one-object category whose endomorphism monoid is `M` (with `End (SingleObj.star M) ≃ M`), realizing the monoid–category correspondence.

annotations.label: Diagram

anchor.snippet: a diagram of type J in C is a covariant functor

annotations.note: A diagram of shape J is a functor `J ⥤ C`, the representation Mathlib uses throughout the limits library.

annotations.label: Presheaf

anchor.snippet: a J -presheaf on C is a contravariant functor

annotations.note: A presheaf is a contravariant functor, represented in Mathlib as `Cᵒᵖ ⥤ D`.

annotations.label: Presheaves on a space

anchor.snippet: Contravariant functors on Open( X ) are called presheaves on X

annotations.note: `TopCat.Presheaf X` is defined as `(Opens X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ C`, exactly a presheaf on the space X.

annotations.label: Constant functor

anchor.snippet: Such a functor is called a constant or selection functor

annotations.note: `Functor.const J : C ⥤ (J ⥤ C)` sends each object to the constant functor at it.

annotations.label: Endofunctor

anchor.snippet: A functor that maps a category to that same category

annotations.note: An endofunctor is a functor `C ⥤ C`; Mathlib uses these pervasively (e.g. for monads) without a dedicated type.

annotations.label: Identity functor

anchor.snippet: The identity functor is an endofunctor

annotations.note: `Functor.id C` (notation `𝟭 C`) is the identity endofunctor.

annotations.label: Diagonal functor

anchor.snippet: The diagonal functor is defined as the functor

annotations.note: `Functor.diag : C ⥤ C × C` is the diagonal functor; the general diagonal into a functor category is `Functor.const`.

annotations.label: Limit functor

anchor.snippet: assigns to each functor its limit

annotations.note: `Limits.lim : (J ⥤ C) ⥤ C` assigns to each diagram its limit (given `HasLimitsOfShape`).

annotations.label: Power set functor

anchor.snippet: The power set functor P : Set → Set maps each set to its power set

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Set.powerset` but no categorical power-set functor `Type ⥤ Type` with its action on functions.

annotations.label: Dual space functor

anchor.snippet: The map which assigns to every vector space its dual space

annotations.note: `Module.Dual` with the contravariant action `LinearMap.dualMap` (and `dualMap_id`/`dualMap_comp_dualMap`) exists, but is not packaged as a category-theoretic functor on a category of vector spaces.

annotations.label: Fundamental group functor

anchor.snippet: Consider the category of pointed topological spaces

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the fundamental groupoid functor `TopCat ⥤ Grpd` (and `FundamentalGroup`), but not the pointed π₁ : Top* ⥤ Grp described here.

annotations.label: Continuous functions functor

anchor.snippet: A contravariant functor from the category of topological spaces

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `C(X, ℝ)` (`ContinuousMap`) but no contravariant functor on a category of spaces sending X to its ring of continuous functions.

annotations.label: Tangent bundle functor

anchor.snippet: The map which sends every differentiable manifold to its tangent bundle

annotations.note: `TangentBundle` is defined, but there is no category of smooth manifolds nor a tangent-bundle functor in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Group as category / G-set

anchor.snippet: Every group G can be considered as a category with a single object

annotations.note: A group G is realized as a one-object groupoid `SingleObj G`; G-sets correspond to functors `SingleObj G ⥤ Type` (cf. `CategoryTheory.Action`).

annotations.label: Lie algebra functor

anchor.snippet: Assigning to every real (complex) Lie group its real (complex) Lie algebra defines a functor

annotations.note: The Lie algebra of a Lie group is constructed via left-invariant derivations (`Mathlib.Geometry.Manifold.GroupLieAlgebra`), but not packaged as a functor from a category of Lie groups.

annotations.label: Tensor product functor

anchor.snippet: defines a functor C × C → C which is covariant in both arguments

annotations.note: `MonoidalCategory.tensor : C × C ⥤ C` is the tensor bifunctor (covariant in both arguments) for any monoidal category, e.g. `ModuleCat`.

annotations.label: Forgetful functor

anchor.snippet: are termed forgetful functors

annotations.note: `forget C : C ⥤ Type` (and `forget₂` between concrete categories) are the forgetful functors.

annotations.label: Free functor

anchor.snippet: Going in the opposite direction of forgetful functors are free functors

annotations.note: Free functors left-adjoint to forgetful functors are formalized, e.g. `MonCat.free : Type ⥤ MonCat` with its `adj` adjunction.

annotations.label: Hom functor (abelian groups)

anchor.snippet: To every pair A , B of abelian groups one can assign the abelian group Hom( A , B )

annotations.note: The hom-bifunctor `Functor.hom : Cᵒᵖ × C ⥤ Type` is formalized for any category; the Ab-internal-hom-valued version is a special case.

annotations.label: Representable functor

anchor.snippet: Functors like these are called representable functors

annotations.note: `Functor.IsRepresentable` (with data `Functor.RepresentableBy`) captures functors `Cᵒᵖ ⥤ Type` naturally isomorphic to a `yoneda.obj X`.

annotations.label: Functor category

anchor.snippet: The collection of all functors from C to D forms the objects of a category

annotations.note: `Functor.category` is the category structure on `C ⥤ D` whose morphisms are natural transformations.

annotations.label: Functor in Haskell

anchor.snippet: the programming language Haskell has a class Functor

annotations.note: The Haskell-style `Functor` typeclass (`fmap`) is a programming construct; Lean's core `Functor` class lives outside `Mathlib/`, so it is not present in Mathlib.
